questions.q: A pod has `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` nodeAffinity but no nodes match. What happens?
options: The pod schedules on any available node
The pod stays in Pending until a matching node is available
The pod is rejected and returns an error immediately
The scheduler upgrades the constraint to preferred
questions.explanation: requiredDuringScheduling is a hard constraint. If no matching node exists, the pod stays Pending. It will NOT fall back to scheduling on any node.

questions.q: Which node affinity operator would you use to schedule a pod only on nodes that DO NOT have the label `size=small`?
questions.explanation: NotIn excludes nodes where the label value matches the list. Using `key: size, operator: NotIn, values: [small]` ensures the pod avoids nodes labeled size=small.

questions.q: In a `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` rule, what does the `weight` field control?
options: The minimum number of nodes that must match
The CPU resource weight allocated to the pod
The relative priority of this preference when scoring nodes
The timeout before the preference is ignored
questions.explanation: Weight (1-100) determines how much a preference contributes to the node score. Higher weight preferences influence the final node selection more strongly.

questions.q: You want to guarantee ML workloads ONLY run on GPU nodes AND prevent non-ML pods from using GPU nodes. Which combination achieves this?
questions.explanation: Taints prevent non-GPU pods from landing on GPU nodes. Tolerations allow GPU pods past the taint. Node Affinity (required) guarantees GPU pods actually go to GPU nodes — not just any non-tainted node.

questions.q: Which operator in node affinity lets you match a node that has a label key regardless of its value?
questions.explanation: The Exists operator matches any node that has the specified label key, regardless of the value. No 'values' list is needed when using Exists.
